About: It is about warfare in general during World War II, and naval warfare in particular, as well as the use of industry in warfare.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with warfare during World War II.
It is associated with naval warfare.
It is associated with the use of industry in warfare.
ComposedBy: It was composed by Richard Rodgers.
CreatedBy: It was created by Henry Salomon.
HasA: It has excerpts from the music soundtrack.
It has a condensed film version released in 1954.
It has a feature-length motion picture condensation.
It has a 79-minute condensation for broadcast.
It has commentary tracks by film historian Peter C. Rollins.
It has 12 short piano compositions by Richard Rodgers.
It has orchestrating by Robert Russell Bennett.
It has "Special Effect Battle Sounds" as part of many of the tracks.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by its compilation documentary genre.
It is characterized by its music soundtrack.
It is characterized by its historical footage.
Influenced: It influenced historic "compilation" documentaries as a television genre.
IsA: It is a documentary television series.
It is a hit on the pop charts.
It is in the public domain.
It is released on VHS, Betamax, CED, LaserDisc, DVD and Blu-ray.
KnownFor: It is known for its depiction of warfare during World War II.
It is known for its naval warfare focus.
It is known for its compilation documentary style.
It is known for its music score by Richard Rodgers and Robert Russell Bennett.
OccurredIn: It occurred in the United States.
PartOf: It is part of NBC's Project 20 series.
PerformedBy: It was performed by members of the NBC Symphony Orchestra.
It was performed by members of the Symphony of the Air.
It was performed by the RCA Victor Symphony Orchestra.
It was performed by the New York Philharmonic.
ProducedBy: It was produced by Henry Salomon.
PublishedAs: It was published as a film released in 1954.
It was published as a feature-length motion picture condensation.
It was published as a 79-minute condensation for broadcast.
It was published as a three-disc Blu-ray set.
It was published as two DVDs and a bonus disc.
PublishedBy: It was published by Embassy Home Entertainment.
It was published by The History Channel/New Video.
It was published by Periscope Film.
It was published by Mill Creek Entertainment.
It was published by Columbia Records.
It was published by RCA Victor.
PublishedIn: It was published in the United States.
It was published in forty foreign markets.
UsedAs: It is used as a compilation documentary.
It is used as "best public affairs program".
It is used as a television genre.
It is used as "Number One" hit on the pop charts.
UsedFor: It is used for record albums.
UsedIn: It is used in 1952–53.
It is used in 1954.
It is used in 1953.
It is used in 1960.
It is used in 1955.
It is used in 1964.
It is used in forty foreign markets.
It is used in the Rodgers Collection at the Library of Congress.
It is used in Bennett's holograph scores.
It is used in the Library of Congress.
It is used in Northwestern University.
It is used in RCA Victor recordings.
It is used in Columbia Records.
It is used in LP and CD releases.
It is used in VHS, Betamax, CED, LaserDisc, DVD and Blu-ray releases.
It is used in the 2nd Annual Grammy Awards.
It is used in the 1953 musical Me and Juliet.
UsedTo: It is used to depict warfare during World War II.
It is used to depict naval warfare.
It is used to depict the use of industry in warfare.
It is used to establish historic "compilation" documentaries as a television genre.
It is used to re-record excerpts from the music soundtrack.
It is used to create a feature-length motion picture condensation.
It is used to create another, 79-minute, condensation for broadcast.
It is used to play at Richard Nixon's funeral.
